chapters.text: Hi, my name is Mehmet Özkaya and welcome to my course DotNet backend bootcamp including Modular Architecture, Vertical Slice Architecture, Domain Driven Design Secrets and Outbox pattern. I am a software architect and currently focusing on dotnet module monitor architectures and implementations. So in this course we are going to learn modular monoliths on dotnet platform which used ASP.Net web API, PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ, Keycloak stack, Docker, Masstransit, Entity Framework, Core Secrets, mediator, Carter, domain driven design, vertical slice and outbox pattern implementations. So now let's start with understanding why we choose modular monolith architecture for dotnet backend bootcamp. In the early days of the software development, monolithic architectures were the standard for all components of an application, from user interface to processing data management, were included within a single indivisible unit that is simplified initial development and deployment because everything is centralized. However, as applications grew in size and complexity, the challenges multiplied. Updating a single part of the system often required redeploying the entire application, which could introduce the risk and downtime. Scaling became inefficient as it required to scale the entire application, even if only one part was needed to scale, and in the response. The limitations of the monolithic architectures. The microservices architecture emerged as a paradigm shift, and particularly for the large scale enterprise applications, and this approach decompose an application into small, independently deployable services, each running in its own process and communicating via a lightweight mechanism such as the HTTP APIs. Microservice architectures offer improved scalability, flexibility, and resilience, making it easier to update and scale parts of an application independently without impacting the whole application. But despite its advantages, the microservices approach introduces its own set of challenges. The distributed nature of the microservices leads to complexities in managing network latency, data consistency and fault tolerance. Moreover, it is complicating the development environment and operational overhead, including monitoring, logging and security across separated services. As the industry continues to wrestle with the tradeoffs between monolithic and microservice architectures. A middle path has started to gain favor, which is the bridge between traditional monolithic and microservice architectures. Which name is the modular monolith architecture? Or we can shortly say modulate architectures. So why we choose modular monolith architecture, modular monolith combine the strengths of the monolithic structure with the flexibility of microservices, and this architecture breaks down a traditional monolith into well-defined, loosely coupled modules. Each module is designed around a business domain similar to microservices, but they all run within the same application process, avoiding the complexity of the network communication between services. One of the key advantages is simplifying data management and maintaining transaction integrity with a unified or coordinated database schema. Running all components within the same process eliminates the network overhead seen in microservice architectures. A significant benefit of the modular monolith approach is its evolutionary nature. Organizations can start with a modular monolith, and as needs evolve, gradually convert specific modules into microservices. This flexibility is crucial for businesses facing rapidly changing markets or undergoing digital transformation. So the rise of the modular monoliths reflects the industry's ongoing quest for the optimal software architectures by balancing development efficiency, operational simplicity, and scalability. Modular monoliths offer an adaptive and resilient model suitable for today's dynamic business environment. For that reason, we choose modular monolith architecture for our. Net backend project. Here you can find our big picture of the course project. During the course, we are going to develop each modular architecture on the. Net platforms step by step and together. There is a couple of modules which implemented each modular monolith architecture. Those are the catalogue, basket ordering and identity modules. And if you look at the data management, the data for these modules will be stored in a PostgreSQL relational databases and Redis distributed cache. While we use a single PostgreSQL database, each module will have its own database schema to ensure data isolation between modules. If you look at the architecture principles, we will follow the circles and vertical slice architecture for each module. Development modules will communicate with each other over the in-process method calls. That means we will expose public APIs and we also use RabbitMQ for event driven asynchronous communication. We will secure our APIs with Keycloak using OpenID Connect and Bearer tokens. We will implement the outbox pattern for reliable messaging between modules. chapters.text: In this section, we are going to learn data management of the modular monolith architectures, and we will set up the PostgreSQL database as a main database. Here you can see that we are at this point, and we will learn how modules store their data into a single relational PostgreSQL databases. So in this section we will learn data management of the modular monolith architectures. And we will learn database schema separation of the modules in module monolith applications. And we will learn what is backing services and how we can use backing services with our modular monoliths. And we will learn Docker compose, run baking services and databases. And we will add the PostgreSQL database as a main database of our application into Docker compose file. And we will learn deployment strategy of each of modular monolith application. We will set up the PostgreSQL db using docker compose file and configuration for multi-container docker environment. And lastly, we will run the Docker compose on Visual Studio in order to set up PostgreSQL db on Docker environment. In this video we are going to learn data management of modular monolith applications. We will explore data isolation strategies, why data isolation is crucial, and the best practices we should follow for our Escape modular monolith application. So let me start with the why is data isolation is important. Data isolation is key aspect of modular monoliths for several reasons. The first one is maintainability. Isolating data per module makes the system easier to maintain and evolve. The second reason is scalability. Different parts of the system can scale independently. The third reason is security. Sensitive data can be isolated and protected more effectively. And another reason is conflict reduction that reduces the risk of data conflicts between modules and ensuring the data integrity. And after that, we will learn data isolation strategies in modular monolith projects. We will discuss four primary data isolation approaches used in the modular monoliths. These are the separate table, separate schema separate database and using the different persistence layers. So every approach has benefits and drawbacks. Now let me explain. One by one we will start with the separate table. In this approach each module has its own set of tables within a single database. There are several advantages to using separate tables. These are the simple to implement and manage and easy to query across modules. But it also comes with some of these advantages. These are the limited isolation schema. Change in one module can affect the other modules, and it is hard to enforce boundaries leading to potential data leakage. And after that we can go to the next strategy, which is the separate schema. In this strategy, each module has its own schema within the same database. And it has some advantages. It is a good balance between isolation and manageability. It is logical separation of data, making it clear which data belongs to which module, and it is easy to enforce boundaries and access controls at the schema level. And there are some disadvantages. It is more complex than separate tables, but still manageable, and shared database resources can become a bottleneck and the next strategy is separate the databases. Each module uses its own database. It has some advantages. These are the excellent isolation and changes in one database do not affect the others, and independent scaling and maintenance for each database can be available in this strategy, but it comes with disadvantages. It is increased complexity in management and backup, and it is higher operational cost due to multiple database instances and Cross-module queries become more complex and may require inter database communication mechanisms, and the last strategy is different persistence mechanisms. Each module can use different type of storage technologies like SQL, NoSQL and other technologies for the data persistence. So it comes with the advantages. Modules can choose the best storage technology for their specific needs and high degree of isolation and flexibility, and these advantages are significant. Increase in complexity and operational overhead. Difficult to manage and maintain consistency across different storage mechanisms. In this video, we are going to learn best practices when database schema separation of modules in the modular monolith architectures. So let me start with the why is database schema separation is important in a modular monolith architecture, maintaining clear boundaries between different modules is essential. Database schema separation is a practice that helps to achieve this by isolating the data of each module within its own schema, So the best practice is separating schema strategy. For each module monolith application we will follow the separate schema approach. So why separate schema approach is the best practices. Because it provides logical data isolation. Each modules data is stored in a separate schema, making it clear which data belongs to which module this logical separation helps to maintain the integrity and consistency of the data, and it also provides a good balance between isolation and ease of management and ensures logical separation of data, which simplifies the maintenance and security. You can see the image on the slide. This is following the separate schema approach in a single PostgreSQL database and separating schema as per the modules. These are the catalog basket and ordering. So the second reason is ease of management. Easier to manage compared to multiple databases or different persistent mechanisms by isolating data, schema separation reduces the complexity of managing the database. Also, changes in one module schema do not impact the other modules, making it easier to evolve and maintain the application simplifies the backup, restore, and migration processes. Another reason is performance. It reduces the complexity of cross-module queries. Shared database resources can be optimized more effectively. Another reason is security. It allows for fine grained access control at the schema level and simplifies the auditing and compliance requirements. So now we will see how we can implement separate schema approach in our E-shop modular monolith architecture. Here you can see the image during the course. We will follow separate schema data isolation strategy for our E-shop modulate application in our E-shop modular monolith application. Each module example, catalog, basket ordering and identity will have its own data schema within a single PostgreSQL database, and this approach will help us to maintain a clear separation of data while keeping management overhead is low. So here you can see that we will develop a submodule monoliths with these modules, and we will use a single PostgreSQL database with separating the schemas as per the modules. But how can we provide these schema separation in our application. We will see it in the next video. In this video we are going to learn Multi-context Entity framework Dbcontext object for database. Separate schema approach. In module monolith architecture, we aim to isolate data for each module. One effective way to achieve this by using the separate schema approach within a single database. Entity Framework Core allows us to configure separate schema approach easily and ensuring each modules data is isolated, enhancing maintainability, security and scalability. We will change the schema name as per the are ordering database schema name. So we demonstrated how to configure multiple Dbcontext object to implement schema isolation using the Entity Framework core. In this video we are going to learn baking services for cloud native architectures. Baking services are the external resources or components that cloud native applications depend on for their operation. They provide support for various functionality such as data storage, messaging, caching, and authentication in a cloud native architecture. Baking services like databases, messaging systems, and caching services are treated as attached resources. These services are external to the application and can be swapped or replaced without changing the application's core logic. In cloud native architecture, backing services are decoupled from the application themselves, promoting the flexibility, scalability and easier maintenance. Here you can see some of the common backing services you might encounter in a cloud native applications. In this image you can see the banking services including the databases, caching, message brokers, event streaming, object storage, authentication, security API gateways, service matching, logging, monitoring and search, and other things. So you can see databases, SQL and NoSQL databases like PostgreSQL and MongoDB that are used for persistent storage as a backing services. And you can see distributed caches Services like Redis or Memcached are used to cache data, reducing load on the database and improving the performance. And you can see the message brokers systems like RabbitMQ, Kafka or AWS SQS facilitate asynchronous communication between different parts of the application and decoupling them and allowing them for more scalable architectures. And you can see authentication services tools like Keycloak or AWS Cognito handle the authentication and authorization and ensuring the secure access to services. So these are the backing services that we can use during the course. And here you can see the backing services for our Icap modular monolith application is a modular monolith using the PostgreSQL. And this will be our main and single database as a backing services. And beside the PostgreSQL we use other backing baking services to support different modules. So for example we will use Redis for basket module. Redis is used a distributed cache to improve the performance and scalability to the basket module. And you can see RabbitMQ for messaging. RabbitMQ also facilitate asynchronous communication between modules, ensuring reliable messaging and decoupling different parts of the application. And here you can see the Keycloak for the identity module. So Keycloak handles the authentication and authorization providing secure access management across the application within the identity module. So we can say that banking services are vital for the operation and scalability for our Icap module monitor application. And with leveraging the external resources like the PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ and Keycloak, we ensure our application remains modular, flexible and scalable. chapters.text: In this video, we are going to learn how to dispatch domain events using the Entity Framework Core Savechanges interceptor. It is a crucial step when implementing domain driven design in e-commerce applications like ordering microservices. But before that, let's remember what is domain events in domain driven design? Domain events represent something that happened in the past and the other parts of the same service boundary, same domain need to react to these changes. A domain event is a business event that occurs within the domain model and achieve consistency between aggregates in the same domain, and when any order placed the order placed event might be triggered, it triggered side effects or notify other parts of the system about changes within the domain. And also we can see how to use domain events in domain driven design. It is basically encapsulate the event details and dispatch them to the interested parties and communicate changes within the domain to external handlers, which may be perform actions based on these events. And here you can see each of the domain events in ordering microservices. When a customer place an order a domain event, which name is ordered created, domain event is raised that will trigger several things need to happen like updating inventory, notifying the shipping department, and sending an email for confirmation to the customer. So all these actions can be triggered by throwing an order created domain event from the order aggregate. So how we can dispatch these domain events. How we can raise and dispatching domain events into our application. So when we talk about the raising the event within the order aggregate, when order new order placed and it's adding an order created domain event to the collection of the domain event in the order aggregate. So the question is how to dispatching these domain events. So in order to dispatching this event, this will be typically done in the infrastructure layer and often in the repository or unit of work. That means after saving to do changes into database, the domain events are dispatched to their respective handlers. You can see that we have started with the entity. In our case, this will be the order aggregate that include domain events list. So whenever any order created this will be triggered order created domain event and adding this domain event into list of the domain events into the aggregate. And after that there is a separate event dispatcher. In our case this will be using Entity Framework code save change interceptor in order to dispatch these domain events to the event consumers. So why we use Entity Framework Core. Save change interceptor for this action because it is a best practice to use these entity Framework core. Save change interceptor for dispatching domain events because it allows you to hook into the life cycle of database operations, particularly after changes have been successfully committed. So after deciding to using these Savechanges interceptor, we can go to the steps when implementing dispatching domain events. The first step is create a safe change interceptor class. We will implement a class that inherits from the Savechanges interceptor and override methods like saving changes or saving changes. Async. And the second step is identify and dispatch domain events. In the override methods, we can check the Dbcontext for entities that have unprocessed domain events, and after that we will dispatch these events using a mechanism like mediator. That means we will use mediator library when dispatching these domain events. And after that you can see we will use domain Eventdispatcher interceptor class to perform these operations and inherit from the Savechanges interceptor. And lastly we will register the interceptor. In this step we will register the interceptor in the dependency injection class of our infrastructure layer. So dispatching domain events using the Entity Framework core Savechanges interceptor is a powerful approach to handling business operations that are triggered by the changes in the domain. In this video we are going to develop Dispatch Domain Events Interceptor to trigger domain event handlers using mediator in the shared library. But before that, let's understand what is Entity Framework core migrations. Entity framework core migrations are a powerful feature for managing database schema changes in an application's life cycle. They enable a developer to evolve a database schema in a controlled and consistent way, in line with the domain model. Changes in real world projects. Data models change. As features get implemented, new entities or properties are added and removed, and database schemas need to be changed accordingly to be kept in sync with the application. Migrations provide a way to incrementally update the database schema so that it stays in synchronous with the application's data model. It is especially useful in team environments and continuous deployment scenarios, where database schema changes are frequent and need to be applied systematically. So how Entity Framework core migration works when a data model change occurs, developers use Entity Framework core tools in order to create a corresponding migration folder that describe the necessary database updates. Entity Framework Core compares the current model against a snapshot of the previous model, in order to find differences and generate migration source files. Once a new migration has been generated, it can be applied to a database in various ways. Entity framework Core records all applied migrations in a special history table, allowing it to know which migrations has been applied and which haven't. So we will use Entity Framework core code first approach in our application. In this video we are going to learn skills. Design pattern skills is one of the important pattern when querying between microservices. We can use secure design pattern in order to avoid complex queries to get rid of the inefficient joins. Seacrest stands for the command and Query Responsibility segregation. And basically this pattern separates read and write operations with separating the databases. The main idea is that we can divide application operations into two separate categories. The first one is commands. These commands are changing the state of data into application, and the second one is queries. And these queries are handling the complex. Join operations and returning a result and don't change the state of data into the application. In a large scale, microservice architectures need to manage high volume data requirements. For this kind of use cases, monolithic approach like single databases for services, can cause the bottlenecks for complex and large scale microservice architectures. We can use both sequence and event sourcing pattern in order to improve application performance. SQS offers to separate the read and write data that provide to maximize query performance and scalability. Let's think about the monolithic applications. Most of the time we have one database, and this database should respond to both query and update operations. That means a single database is both working for the complex join queries and also perform Crud operations. But if the application grows more complex, this query and Crud operations will be also is going to be unmanageable situation. In example of reading database, if your application required some queries that needs to join more than one table that will lock the database due to latency of query computation. Also, if we give example of writing database when performing Crud operations, we would need to make complex validations and process long business logics. So this will cause to lock the database operations also. So reading and writing database has different approaches that we can define different strategy in order to handle that operations. In order to do that, Sickles offer to use separation of concerns principles and separate reading database and writing database with two databases. By this way, we can even use different database for reading and writing database types, like using NoSQL databases for reading operations and using relational databases for Crud operations. Mostly read database uses NoSQL database with denormalized data which is generated from the right database, and right database uses relational databases with fully normalized and support strong data consistency. In this video, we are going to learn logical and physical implementation of the secretase. Secretase can be implemented in two main ways. These are the logical and physical implementation. Let me start with the logical implementation of secretase. We will splitting the operations, not databases. In a logical implementation of secretase, the key idea is separate read and write operations at the code level, but not necessarily at the database level. So that means we will separate the query and command operations at the code level and not separate with the database level. Both types of operations still interact with the same underlying database. So we said that it is the same database but different paths. Even though the same database is used, the path for reading and writing data are distinct. This means that you might have different models or methods for querying and updating data. For instance, read operations could utilize the simple data transfer object optimized for performance, while write operations might interact with a more complex domain model to ensure business rules and validations are enforced. So the second type of the implementation is physical implementation of the queries. A physical implementation of the sequence involves splitting the read and write operations, not just at the code level, but also physically using the separate databases. This is often seen in a more complex systems, particularly when scaling is a concern. So there is a synchronization challenge happen when we are separate databases. While a physical implementation can offer performance advantages and better scaling capabilities, it also introduces complexity in terms of the maintaining data consistency and synchronization between the read and write operations. So you can see that if we separated write and read databases, we have to concern about the synchronization challenges. That's why logical security is often the first step in adopting this security pattern, especially for applications where the overhead of managing separate databases is not justified. And now we are at this point and develop cross-cutting concerns for catalog and other modules in our Icap Modular Monolith application. So this module we will learn mediator pipeline behaviors. And we will apply validation with abstract validator using different validation library. And after that we will use validation pipeline behavior with mediator using the full length validation library to optimize these validate operations. And we will use global handling exceptions in ASP.Net core with using application use exception handler extension method. And after that we will use logging behavior in mediator pipeline for cross-cutting concerns. And we will develop Getproducts query. Apply the pagination using the cookies and mediator. And after developing shared cross-cutting concerns in the shared library, we will implement these under the catalog module in order to apply all cross-cutting concerns into the catalog module. In this video we are going to learn mediator, pipeline behavior and fluent validation library. Mediator is a popular library in dotnet used for implementing the mediator pattern. One of its powerful feature is pipeline behaviors, which allows us to introduce additional logic into the request handling process such as validation, logging, exception handling, and performance tracking. Pipeline behaviors act as a middleware in the mediator library. They wrap around the request handling process, enabling us to implement cross-cutting concerns without cluttering the handlers themselves. A pipeline behavior is essentially a class that implements I pipeline behavior interface with t request and t response. It provides the handle method where you can execute the code before and after the next delegate is invoked. Here you can see that how mediator pipeline behaviors works. You can see that mediator will be triggered handle method. But before that we can invoke the preprocessor behavior. And after that you can invoke the post-processor behavior. So you can see an example of that. The request comes into the first middleware and perform the first logic. And after that invoke the next method in order to pass the middleware to and middleware to also implement the actual logic and invoke the next middleware with next method, and after that, and middleware three is the last middleware that performs the actual logic and response back to the middleware two and one. That means this will be invoke post-process behaviors and return back to the response. So here you can see another example which is the tracing behavior. So in the pipeline behavior this is tracing the logic. And after that invoking await next method. And this will be handled actual handler method. And after that this will goes to post pipeline behavior. And this will be traced after operation under the post processor behavior. And here you can see basic code example. That is a pipeline behavior that locks the execution time of the request. This is the logging behavior. And in the handle method this will be basically logging the execution time. And after that handle the actual handle logic. So this was the mediator pipeline behaviors. And now we will focus on the fluent validation library and how mediator pipelines work with the fluent validation libraries. What is fluent validation? Fluent validation is a. Net library for building strongly typed validation rules. It is known for its clean syntax and ease of use, making validation logics more readable and maintainable. So we can integrate fluent validation and with mediator to validate requests before they reach the actual handler. This integration happens within a pipeline behavior, especially prior pipeline behaviors. So fluent validation allows you to define validation rules in a separate classes, using a fluent interface to specify conditions that each property of your models must satisfy. You can see the image on the slide. This is an example of validation class for a request model specifying various validation rules. So combining mediator with fluent validation, centralized or cross-cutting concerns like validation, making our code cleaner and more maintainable. It is also ensures that our business logic only process valid data. So these will be the an example. This is working preprocessor that is validating the create product command and checking the members name category image file price. And these should not be empty and the price should be greater than zero. So why we need to develop cross-cutting concerns? Because cross-cutting concerns are aspects of a program that affect other concerns. They often represent system wide requirements such as logging, validation, error handling, and security. In a modular monolith architecture, it is efficient to centralize these common concerns into the shared library so that they can be reused across different modules, and this approach minimizes the redundancy and ensure consistency across the application. And before we proceed, it is good to understand and remember our project folder structure. As you remember that we have developed our feature folder as a basket under the basket module. And this time we will focus on the data folder. And in data folder we will develop configurations migrations, repository related interactions in order to communicate with the PostgreSQL database from our basket module. And if you look at our separated schema in the module monitor architecture, as you remember that we have separated schema for each module inside of the are escape modular monolith application. That means we provide the data isolation, separating the schemas into our PostgreSQL database and following the. Each module has its own schema. So how we can provide to create each module has its own schema. Of course we will use multi context Entity Framework core Dbcontext for separating our schemas. That means in our modular monolith application, each module can have its own Dbcontext object, and this setup provides data isolation and help in maintaining clean separation of the database. And each Dbcontext object can be configured to use different schema within a single relational database. chapters.title: Monolithic Architecture - What's wrong with Traditional Monoliths
files: 10. Big Ball of Mud.en_US.srt
7. What is Traditional Monoliths (Monolithic Architecture).en_US.srt
8. What's wrong with Traditional Monolithic Architecture.en_US.srt
9. Technology Lock-In and Fear Cycle in Traditional Monolithic Architecture.en_US.srt
chapters.text: In this video, we are going to explore the concept of the big ball of mud. A big ball of mud refers to a software system with a messy and poorly structured architecture. It's a system where the code is so entangled that is hard to understand, maintain or extend. A big ball of mud has no clear structure or organization. Everything is mixed together, making it hard to figure out where one part ends and another begins. Over time, quick fixes and shortcuts add up, creating technical depth. This makes the code messy and difficult to work with. Several factors can lead to a become a big ball of mud. The first one is rapid development cycles. In a rush to deliver features quickly, best practices are often ignored. This leads to a messy codebase full of shortcuts, and another reason can be lack of proper design. Without proper design and planning, the system evolves haphazardly. Design decisions are made on the fly and leading to inconsistencies. And we can also say inadequate refactoring. Over time, code needs to be cleaned up and improved. If this doesn't happen regularly, the code base becomes hard to manage. There are many approaches and patterns that evolved over decades of software development, and all have their own benefits and challenges. In this section, we will learn traditional monoliths, which is the monolithic architecture. And what's wrong with the traditional monoliths. So let me start with the what is monolithic architecture? Monolithic architecture is the traditional way of building applications. In a monolithic application, all components and functionalities are packaged into a single cohesive unit. This means that the user interface, business logic and data access layers are all contained within one application. If you build everything together and deploy it on the server, that's called a monolithic architecture where the services are tightly coupled together. When it comes to legacy applications, we can say that most of the legacy applications are mainly implemented as a monolithic architecture. In the monolithic pattern, all application concerns are contained in a single big deployment. Even the monolithic applications can design in different layers like presentation, business and data layer, and then deploy that code base as a single jar or war file. Developing your application as a monolithic architecture might seem like going backward in the architectural evolution, but in some cases monolithic architecture still best option to design your application architecture, especially when you require to strong consistency. So now let's check the characteristics of the monolithic architecture. And the first characteristic is of course single code base. All codes reside in a single code base, making it easier to manage and deploy. Initially, there is no separation between different parts of the application that leading to tightly coupling, and the second characteristic is single deployment unit. The entire application is packaged and deployed as a single unit. This can simplify the deployment process, especially in the early stages of the development. And another characteristic is tight coupling. Components within the application are tightly coupled, meaning changes in one part of the application can affect the others. This can lead to increased complexity and difficulty in making changes or scaling specific parts of the application. And another characteristic is shared database. Typically, a monolithic application uses a single shared database for all its data needs. This can lead to challenges in maintaining and scaling the databases as the applications grow. In this video, we are going to learn what is wrong with the traditional monolithic architecture. In many ways, monolithic applications are straightforward. However, multi-core architectures can cause many problems. One of the most significant problems with traditional multi-core architecture is scalability. In multicore architecture, the entire application is a single unit. This means that when one part of the application needs to scale, the entire application must be scaled. You can scale components independently. The only option is to scaling the whole application. You can scale individual components. For example, if the user authentication component experiences high load, you can scale just that component. You need to scale the entire application, leading to inefficient resource utilization. Scaling a monolithic application can be resource, incentive, and costly. You might end up using more resources than necessary, as you can fine tune the scaling for individual components. And another problem is development bottlenecks. As the application grows, development can slow down due to the bottlenecks. And the first bottleneck is complex code base. It becomes too large in size with time and that's why it is difficult to manage. application growth with additional new functionalities. A monolithic code base can become extremely large and complex. This can be difficult to manage. It becomes too complicated to understand, especially as the team of the developers working on the individual code base expands. So that means at some point, the monolithic application has become so complicated that no single person understands it. And another problem is tightly coupling components in a multicore architecture are tightly coupled. Chains in one part of the application can have unintended consequences in other parts. If we expand these tightly coupling problem, we can say that it is harder to implement new chains in such a large and complex application with highly tight coupling, any code chains affect the whole system. A large monolithic application makes development more complex and slower. This makes you fear when making new changes, and these changes brings costly side effects. And also this makes the overall development process much longer. That means developing new features become time consuming and expensive to implement. Even the smallest change requires full deployment of the entire application. It is expensive and risky. It is not reliable because a single bug can bring down the whole monolithic application. And if you contain the problems, we can say that deployment risk, monolithic application face significant risk during the deployment. And the first one is single point of failure since the entire application is deployed as a single unit, any issue during the deployment can bring down the entire system. There is no isolation between components and difficult to rollback. Rollback A monolithic application can be challenging. If a deployment fails, you must rollback the entire application, which can be complex and risky process, and the last one is frequent downtime. Deploying updates to the monolithic application often requires downtime. This can impact the user experience, especially if updates are frequent. Traditional monolithic architectures can limit your ability to adopting new technologies and responding to changing requirements. So we can say that when all components are tightly integrated, it is extremely problematic to apply a naive technology in your monolithic application because the entire application has to be redevelopment due to interlocking dependencies found in a monolithic. For example, if you want to use a different database technology for a particular component, it can be difficult to make this change without affecting the entire application. Any changes in the framework or language affects the entire application, making changes often expensive and time consuming. We can say this condition as a fear cycle. If you have developing same monolithic application for a long time, it is stressful to change your full code base with the newest technology instead of building new or innovative solutions. Most of your time is spent maintaining legacy applications. So we can say that adding new technologies and frameworks aren't an option for monolithic architectures. Responding to new business requirements or market changes can be slow. Any significant change requires through testing and coordination across the entire application. So also we have maintenance challenges. Maintaining a monolithic application over time can be a daunting task, and this can be create a problem for the technical debt. As the application grows, it is easy to accumulate technical debt. These include outdated code, poor documentation, and quick fixes that compromise code quality. And second problem will be bug fixes and enhancements. Fixing bugs and adding new features become increasingly complex. The risk of introducing new bugs while fixing existing ones is requiring extensive testing. And another problem will be team coordination. Coordinating a large team working on a single code base can be difficult. Merging changes from different developers can lead to conflicts and delays.

In this video, we are going to do our last step, which is migrating microservices, evolving contracts, synchronous communication to microservices HTTP calls. We will also explore how the synchronous communication facilitated by contracts class libraries can evolving into microservices HTTP calls. That means we are going to evolving modular monolith communications to microservices communications. So first of all, let's understand the current state Our current state is modular monolith. So we currently have a modular monolith architecture where different modules communicate synchronously using the in-process method calls. Currently, modules use contract class libraries for synchronous inter-process communication. For example, basket module calls the catalog module to get product details using the mediator, and this approach of using contracts, class libraries and synchronous in-process method calls is an excellent practice for modular monolith architectures when migrating to microservices. These contracts can be used to expose HTTP APIs and ensuring seamless transition to the distributed system. That means this kind of contracts class libraries. We will remove this contract class libraries when migrating to microservices and change these public APIs to the HTTP APIs to communicate different microservices. After understanding the current state, we can go to our target state, which is the microservices. So our main goal is to transition to microservice architecture where each module becomes an independent services. For that reason we have separated basket and catalog microservices. And these services communicate over the network using the lightweight protocols like HTTP, Https or gRPC calls. So we will evolving synchronous communication to microservices with HTTP calls. So we have to expose public APIs. That means we convert the contract based communication methods into the RESTful HTTP endpoints. For example, the get product by id query in the catalog module become http get endpoint that we can call from the basket microservices send http get request. And after that we should use service discovery. That means we should use service discovery mechanisms to dynamically locate services. Tools like Console and Eureka can help with this. And also we need an API gateway. We should implement an API gateway to manage and route the requests to the appropriate microservices, and we also refactoring the communication replace in-process method calls with the HTTP client calls. For example, we use HTTP client in the basket module. In order to call Catalog Services API endpoint, we need the HTTP client dotnet packages. And also we need to handle the failures. We should implement resilience patterns like retries, circuit breakers, and fallbacks using libraries like poly. So that means transitioning from a modular monolith architecture to microservices involves several steps, including the exposing public APIs, implementing the service, discovery, and refactoring the communication patterns by evolving synchronous communication facilitated by the contracts class libraries into HTTP calls. We achieve a highly scalable, flexible and resilient system and easy to migrate microservices with, starting with the Modular Modular architecture.

chapters.text: In this section, we are going to develop asynchronous communication between modules with using the RabbitMQ and Masstransit. Here you can see the big picture. We have finished the synchronous communication between catalog and basket modules with in-process method calls. Now it is time to develop asynchronous communication between modules with using the RabbitMQ Masstransit for the update process between catalog and basket module. So here you can see the our internal architecture that we are going to send update price http post request to our catalog module and catalog module. Publishing domain and integration event about the product price change event. This will be published to RabbitMQ and RabbitMQ. Event will consume from the basket module and basket module. Performing internal actions with updating the price into our shopping cart with consuming the price change integration event. So that means in this section, we are going to include developing asynchronous communication between catalog and basket module. And we will examine catalog module domain and integration events. And we will create shared messaging class library and develop product price change integration event. And we will develop mass transit extension methods for abstractions over the RabbitMQ. And we will develop product price change integration event and explain how domain event lead to integration event. And we will publish update price integration event from our catalog module. And in the basket module we subscribe and consume product price change integration event and we use RabbitMQ publish subscribe topic exchange module. And lastly we are going to shift in-memory bus to the RabbitMQ message broker using the mass transit. In this video, we are going to learn how modular monolith architectures handle the asynchronous communication between the modules. So first of all, let's understand asynchronous communication. And why use asynchronous communication. The first reason is decoupling. Asynchronous communication decouples the sender and receiver, allowing them to operate independently. And the second reason is scalability. By not waiting for responses, the system can handle more requests and scale effectively. And another reason is resilience. If a module fails, messages can be retried or processed later, improving the fault tolerance. And another reason is responsiveness. Users receive immediate feedback even if the operation takes time to complete. And the price updates in the catalog module were propagated to Cascade module, and the shopping cart items were updated accordingly. In this video we are going to learn what is RabbitMQ? RabbitMQ is a message broker software that implements the Advanced Messaging Queue protocol, which is the Amqp protocol. It allows applications to communicate with each other by sending and receiving messages through the queues. So RabbitMQ is a message queuing system. Similar ones can be listed as a Apache Kafka, Msmq, Microsoft Azure Service Bus, Kestrel. Activemq and so on. It is proposed to transmit a message received from any source to another source as soon as it is their turn. In other words, all transactions can be listed in a queue until the source to be transmitted gets up. It also allows to send and receive messages asynchronously. This decouples the sender and receiver, allowing each to continue processing without waiting for other to respond. This can improve the overall performance and scalability of the system. RabbitMQ supports for multiple operating systems and open source code in one of the most preferred reasons. The main components of RabbitMQ are producer queue, consumer message exchange, binding and Fifo. So I will explain all these components on the RabbitMQ architecture that you can see on the picture. So let me start with the producer. I will continue with the producer queue and consumer. And after that I will explain the exchange and bindings. So what is producer. Producer is the source of the message for the application. So after that what is queue? Queue is where the messages are stored. The send messages are put in queue before they are received. All incoming messages are stored in the queue. That is the memory. And after that we have consumer. It is a server that meets the send message. It is the application that will receive and process the message on the queue. And also we have a message. Message is the data that we send on the queue. So now I can explain exchange and bindings. So what is the exchange? It is a structure that decides which queue to send the messages. It makes the decision according to the routing case. And also we have bindings. Bindings are the link between the exchange and the queues. And also we can explain the Fifo. What is the Fifo? Fifo is the order of processing of outgoing messages in RabbitMQ. In. First in. chapters.text: In this section we are going to develop user identity module with Keycloak authentication. So that means we have completed catalog and basket module synchronous and asynchronous communication. Now it is time to develop user identity module with Keycloak authentication. Here you can see internal architecture of our identity module. We will use Keycloak as a backing services and identity server. Our application. And we will follow the Keycloak authentication with OpenID connect web token and PostgreSQL database and client will be send request to our identity module in order to retrieve a bearer token and use these token to securing our other modules, including the catalog basket and ordering module. So that means in this section we are going to learn Keycloak Identity and Access management. And we will use Oauth2 and OpenID connect flows with Keycloak. And we will use Keycloak as a backing services of our modular monolith application, and we will set up the Keycloak into our Docker compose file for identity provider as a backing services, and we will set up the User Identity database as a PostgreSQL database identity schema. That means we are going to create a separate schema for these identity module, which name is identity schema. And after that we will create realm user and client for OpenID connect with Keycloak identity provider, and we will secure our Icap modules with using the Keycloak OpenID connect into our ASP.Net API project, and we will use git bearer tokens for OpenID connect with following Keycloak identity, and we are going to get current user from the token with claims principles in ASP.Net core authentication. And after that, we will secure the basket and points with OpenID connect and using the Keycloak identity provider. In this video we are going to learn Keycloak Identity and Access Management. So what is Keycloak? Keycloak is an open source identity and access management solution aimed at modern applications and services. It provides comprehensive support for authentication and authorization, offering features such as single sign on, identity working and social login. Keycloak simplifies the process of securing applications and services with minimal coding effort, and there are key features of the Keycloak. For example, the first one is single sign on. Users can log in once and access multiple applications. And also we can say identity brokering and social login. With using Keycloak, we can integrate with external identity providers like Google, Facebook and so on. And another key feature is user federation. We can connect to existing user databases. And another key feature is centralized management. We can manage all user identities and permissions in one place. And lastly, we can say that it provides standard protocols that support all two OpenID connect and Saml 2.0. So why we use Keycloak? Using Keycloak can significantly reduce the complexity of managing user identities and access controls in our applications, and it offers several benefits, including the security that provides robust security features that adhere to industrial standards, and it provides also scalability that is capable of handling millions of users and requests. chapters.text: In this section we are going to develop outbox pattern for reliable messaging with basket checkout use case, and we will explore how to implement the outbox pattern to ensure reliable messaging between modules in a modular monolith architecture. And our asynchronous use case will be basket checkout process. So here you can see the big picture. As you can see that we have developed our modules and now we will focus on asynchronous communication implementing the outbox pattern. But in a distributed system or even the modular architecture, ensuring reliable communication between different modules or services is crucial. And in the traditional approach we can suffer from the issues like message loss, duplication and inconsistent states when we are communicating between RabbitMQ and modules. In order to solve these issues, we are going to implement outbox pattern. Here you can see the internal architecture of our ESP module monolithic asynchronous communication with following the outbox pattern. In the outbox pattern, messages are stored in a persistent stored before being sent to the message broker, and this guarantees that messages are not lost even if the system crashes. So here you can see that our main use case is basket checkout. And if you follow these end to end process, basically the client sent basket checkout request to our basket module. And this will be accommodated from the basket module. And in the handler method basket perform their operation in a single database transaction. We are performing our business logic. And also we will save entity into our outbox table. After that another process. Read these outbox table and publish the basket Checkout integration event to the RabbitMQ using the mass transit, and this queue typically implements the topic messaging technology and after that subscriber module in our case it will be ordering module will consume this event and create an order in our PostgreSQL database under the ordering schema. chapters.text: In this section, we are going to develop migrating to microservices. Each app modules to microservices with Strangler fig pattern. So we will focus on the application exploring the challenges and considerations and steps required to successfully transition from monolithic architecture to microservice based architecture using the strangler fig pattern. So here you can see the big picture that we have finished the development of our E-shop modular monolith application. And we will move to microservices from our modular application to microservice architecture that we are going to understand the challenges and considerations of this migration. So here you can see that we can extract the identity module, or we can manage our synchronous communication with using the Rest APIs and separating the microservices. But before that let's understand the challenges of migration migrating from monolithic to microservices. That has several challenges. The first one is complexity Splitting a monolithic application into smaller services can introduce significant complexity in terms of architecture, deployment and management. And another challenge comes with the data management. Ensuring data consistency across services is a major concern, particularly when dealing with distributed transactions. And another challenge is inter service communication. Designing efficient communication between services is critical to maintain performance and reliability, and we can say deployment and monitoring problems. Managing multiple services requires robust deployment strategies and comprehensive monitoring solutions. And we can also say team coordination problems migrating to microservices, often necessary changes in team structure and collaboration process. And after that, we can look at the key consideration when moving to our monolithic to microservices. The first one is business priorities. Identify the most critical parts of the application that need to be migrated first, based on business priorities and potential impact. And the second key consideration is domain driven design. We should use domain driven design principles to identify bounded contexts and define the service boundaries. And another consideration is incremental approach. We should adopt an incremental migration strategy to minimize the risks and allow for continuous improvement. And another consideration is technology stack. We should ensure that the technology stack for microservices supports scalability, resilience and maintainability. In this video, we are going to learn Strangler fig pattern for refactoring monolithic into microservices. So what is strangler fig pattern? The Strangler fig pattern is a strategic approach to refactoring monolithic applications into microservices. It emphasizes incremental migration, ensuring minimal disruption to the existing system while gradually introducing the microservices. The pattern is named after the idea of transforming a strangler, which is the monolith, into a fig, which will be the microservices. So why we use the strangler fig pattern? The first reason is to minimize the disruption by incrementally migrating parts of the application. We reduce the risk of the system downtime and user disruption. And the second reason is maintain stability. The existing monolithic application continues to function while individual modules are extracted as microservices. And another reason is scalable transition. The approach allows for a scalable and flexible transition, accommodating changes and improvements throughout the process. And another reason is risk mitigation. Gradual migration allows for slow testing and validation of each microservices, ensuring stability and reliability. So now let's check the considerations for successful migration using the strangler fig pattern. The first one is of course incremental approach. We should adopt an incremental migration strategy to minimize the risks and allow for continuous improvement. Each step should be thoroughly tested and validated before proceeding to the next. And another consideration of course team coordination. We should also ensure effective communication and collaboration among team members. Clearly define the roles and responsibilities in order to streamline the migration process. And another consideration is cultural shift. We should encourage a cultural shift towards the DevOps practices, continuous delivery and automation, and we will foster a mindset of continuous learning and adaptation. And lastly, we can say documentation. We should maintain comprehensive documentation throughout the migration process. These include API documentation, architecture diagrams, and deployment strategies. In this video, we are going to learn steps of refactoring monolith into microservices. Migrating from a monolithic application to microservices involves several key steps. Let's break down the process. Step one is decompose submodules to microservices with domain driven design bounded contexts. Step two is prioritize modules and services for migration. Step three is extract a service from a monolith. Step four is manage a monolithic database. Step five is design and implement inter service communication. Step six is asynchronous communication between services. Step seven is distributed transactions and data consistency. And the last step. Step eight is deployment of microservices after monolithic. So now let's examine these steps one by one. The step one is decomposition modules to microservices with domain driven design bounded context. This will include identifying the bounded context. We should analyze the existing monolithic application to identify the bounded context within the domain. For the application. Modules such as the catalog basket, ordering and identity can be considered as a bounded context, and after that we should define the service boundaries. That means we should define clear boundaries for each microservice based on the identified bounded contexts. chapters.text: In this section we are going to learn microservices architectures, why it is not fit for all projects and what's wrong with the microservices architectures. So now let me start with the what are microservices? Microservices are small, independent and loosely coupled services. So microservices are small business services that can work together and can be deployed independently and autonomously. Each service is a separate code base, which can be managed by a small development team. A single small team of developers can write and maintain a particular microservice. Microservices communicate with each other over the network. Protocols. Services communicate with each other by using well-defined APIs. Internal implementation details of each service are hidden from other services. One of the biggest advantages is that they can be deployed independently. Microservices can be deployed independently. A team can update an existing service without rebuilding and redeploying the entire application. Services do not need to share the same technology stack libraries or frameworks. Microservices can work with many different technology stacks, which is technology agnostic. Microservices has its own database or persistence layer that is not shared with other services. This differs from the traditional model where a separate data layer handles data persistence. So let me continue with the what is microservices architecture. At this point I will get reference from the Martin Fowler article. According to this article, the microservice architecture style is an approach to developing a single application as a suite of small services, each running in its own process and communicating with lightweight mechanisms, often an HTTP or gRPC API. These services are built around business capabilities and independently deployable by fully automated deployment process. There is a bare minimum of the centralized management of these services, which may be written in different programming languages and use different data storage technologies. The microservice architecture is a software design approach that decompose an application into small, independent services that communicate over well-defined APIs. Since each service can be developed and maintained by autonomous teams, it is the most scalable method for software development. These services are owned by small, self-contained teams. Microservices architectures make applications easier to scale and faster to develop, enabling innovation and acceleration. Time to market for the new features. So we can say that microservices architecture is a cloud native architectural approach in which applications composed of many loosely coupled and independently deployable smaller components. Microservices have their own technology stack, including the database and data management model, communicate to each other over a combination of Rest APIs, event streaming and message brokers, and microservices are organized by business capability, with a line of the separating services often referred as a bounded context. In this video, we are going to learn what is wrong with the microservice architecture. While microservices offer many benefits such as the independent scalability and flexibility, they also come with their own set of complexities and issues. One of the primary challenges of microservice architecture is the increased complexity and complexity increased by the distributed systems. Microservices are a distributed system by nature. Managing and coordinating multiple services can be complex. Each service has its own deployment, monitoring and logging, which can be challenging to manage at scale. Since there are lots of services, that means there is more moving parts than the monolithic application. Each service is simpler, but the entire system is more complex. And the second problem is network latency and failures. Services communicate over the network, which introduce latency. Network failures can also disrupt communication between services. Handling network issues required robust retry and fallback mechanism, adding to extra complexity, and also data management in a microservice architecture present a unique challenges and the first problem which comes with the data consistency ensuring data consistency across multiple services and databases is difficult. Traditional asset transactions are hard to implement in a distributed system. Eventual consistency and distributed transactions, for example using the Saga patterns that are often necessary but adding extra complexity. And another problem is data duplication. Data may need to be duplicated across services in order to improve performance and independence. However, this can lead to data inconsistency and increased storage cost. Keeping duplicated data in sync requires additional mechanism such as event sourcing or change data capture. Microservice architecture increased operational overhead which comes with the deployment and management. Deploying and managing multiple services is more complex than handling a single monolithic application. CI CD pipelines need to be set up for each service. Increasing maintenance effort and also monitoring and logging become more complex as you need to aggregate data from multiple services, ensuring end to end visibility and tracing requests across services required. sophisticated monitoring tools. Also, it is required to know DevOps skills. Successful microservice implementation required strong DevOps skills. You must first invest in a quite a lot of DevOps automation processes and tools. Teams need to manage container orchestration tools like Kubernetes and Docker Swarm. DevOps expertise is essential for automating deployment, scaling services and maintaining infrastructure. Managing communication between services is another significant challenge, and these challenges start with the communication protocols. Choosing the right communication protocol for inter service communication is critical, and each protocol has its tradeoffs in terms of the performance, complexity, and ease of use. So since microservices are small and communicate with inter service communication, we should manage network problems. If you call chain of services for particular requests, this will increase latency problems and need correct design to APIs for proper communication. And it also required service discovery. Services need to find and communicate with each other dynamically. Implementing the service discovery mechanism is necessary, but adding more complexity. Tools like console, Eureka and Kubernetes built in service discovery can help, but they require proper configuration and management. And another problem is fault tolerance. We should ensure that microservices can handle failures gracefully. Implementing circuit breakers, retries, and fallback mechanism. Adding to the development effort and complexity. Maintaining resilience of microservice failures require careful planning and robust infrastructure. And the next problem, which comes with the testing and debugging microservices testing and debugging. Microservices can be more complex than with monolithic application. Think about the end to end process, one of the long business requirement if the requirement touch on several microservices that need to act as one application, it is hard to develop and testing these end to end process in microservice architectures if you compare to monolithic application. So that means integration testing as well as the end to end testing can become more difficult and more important than ever. And also debugging distributed systems can be challenging. Tracing a problem across multiple services and logs required good observability tools. Distributed tracing tools like Jaeger and Zipkin can help, but they need to be properly integrated and maintained. Also, remote debugging from your local development environment isn't an option, and it won't work across dozens or hundreds of microservices. Unfortunately, there is no single answer to how to debug at this time. In this video we are going to learn microservices anti-pattern which is the distributed monolith. A distributed monolith is an anti-pattern where a supposedly microservice based system retains the drawbacks of a monolithic architecture. Despite being broken down into separate services. These services are tightly coupled and interdependent, resulting in a system that is difficult to scale, deploy, and maintain. Services are highly dependent on each other. Changes in one service often require changes in other services, making independent deployments nearly impossible. This can slow down development and increase the risk of bugs. Because of the interdependencies, all services need to be deployed together. This neglects one of the main benefits of microservices, which is the ability to deploy independently Multiple services share the same database schema, leading to tight coupling at the data layer. This makes it difficult to change the database schema without affecting the older services. This can lead to data consistency issues and make database migrations challenging. Excessive communication between services indicates that they are not as independent as they should be. Frequent synchronous calls between services can lead to performance bottlenecks. So why these distributed monolithic happens? Understanding why distributed monolithic happens can help us to avoid them. So here you can see some reasons. The first reason is inadequate service boundaries. Poorly defined service boundaries often lead to services that are too interdependent, and identifying the right boundaries for your services is crucial for achieving true microservice architectures. And the second reason could be lack of domain driven design. Without a true understanding of the domain and its subdomains, it is challenging to define services that are cohesive and loosely coupled. Domain driven design helps in creating well-defined service boundaries, and another reason could be database coupling sharing a single database schema across services leads to tight coupling. Each service should ideally have its own database or even own schema in order to maintain independence. And another reason could be legacy code and practices migrating from a monolithic application to microservices without changing legacy code and practices can result in a distributed monolithic. It is important to refactor and rethink the architecture during the migration process. Here you can find very good illustration that explain distributed architectures. If you look at the image, you can see the x axis representing the number of the deployed services and the y axis representing the modularity. So let me start with the traditional monolithic. This is placed in the lower left corner and represent a low modularity. and also representing a small number of the deployed services, and it is indicated a single, large and tightly coupled applications. And if you go ahead, there is a modular monolith which is placed in the middle left and it is moderate the modularity with the moderate number of the deployed services. So it is basically represent a monolithic application divided into well-defined, loosely coupled modules. And if you go here, which is placed in the upper right corner, which is the microservices, and it is representing high modularity and a large number of the deployed services, and it represents an architecture where the functionalities are split into independent services that can be deployed and scaled individually. And if you go here and see the red flag, which is the distributed monolithic, which is placed slightly right of the monolithic application but lower in the modularity. So it is marked as an error with the red outline. And it represents an architecture that has many services but still suffers from the tight coupling and resulting in the same issues as a monolithic architecture despite having multiple services. So this is red flag and anti-pattern of our applications, and we should avoid to be a distributed monolithic. In this video we are going to learn very interesting case study from the Amazon Prime Video. We will see how the Amazon Prime Video team managed to scale up their audio and video monitoring service, while reducing the cost by staggering 90%. So for that purpose, please open the link on the slide and go to the original article. This is the original article written from the Amazon Prime, and in the beginning of the article it is explained to Prime Video that offers thousands of live streams to customers worldwide and ensuring the seamless delivery of content is paramount, which is required robust monitoring of every stream. So in the initial setup, the video quality analyst team had a tool for inspecting audio and video quality, but it wasn't designed for the high scalability as more streams were onboarded. The infrastructure cost skyrocketed and scaling bottlenecks became apparent. So the problem is, running the existing infrastructure at high scale was prohibitively expensive and reached scalability limits. So if you scroll down, you can see the initial distributed microservice architecture in the Amazon Prime. So here you can see the initial architecture. The initial version consisted of distributed components orchestrated by the AWS step functions, and the service has three major components. These are the media converters, defect detectors, and orchestration. So if you look at the cost and scaling issues in the current architecture, the orchestration, workflow and data transfer between distributed components were the main cost drivers each second of stream processing involved multiple state transitions and quickly hitting the AWS account limits and incurring high cost. So you can see the image on the article. So basically each step of the AWS function this is using Amazon S3 and the frequent usage of Amazon S3 for the temporary storage. That becomes a bottleneck and cost. So AWS step functions were used for managing the orchestration, but it is led to high cost and scaling limits due to frequent state transition. So it comes from the data transfer cost. Because video frames were split and temporarily stored in the Amazon S3, with each detector downloading and processing these frames, the high volume of the S3 interactions led to substantial cost, so after that, the team is rethink this architecture and the team decided to re-architect the infrastructure and moving away from the distributed approach to the monolithic architecture. So why this change happened? Because the distributed system wasn't provide the expected benefit for their specific use case. Packing all components into a single process eliminated the need for the intermediate storage and simplified orchestration. So here you can see the newly designed architecture. And this is the new monolithic architecture. And it is showing the control and data plane with all components running within a single Amazon ECS task. And it is showing that how data sharing now happens through instance memory, with only the final result uploaded to S3. So this time the upload operation on the S3 in the final stage, not the transition steps with. Following these monolithic approach, there are some benefits comes from the theme which is eliminated the need for the Amazon S3 as an intermediate storage and reducing the data transfer cost. And it also simplifies the orchestration by keeping it within a single instance, reducing the number of the state transitions. So if you talk about the scaling, you can scroll down and see another illustration about the scaling. While the initial design allowed the horizontal scaling of detectors, the naive approach scales vertically within a single instance. Here you can see the Amazon EKS clusters separating and vertically scaling in here. So in order to handle the capacity of a single instance, the team cloned the service multiple times, each with a subset of the detectors, so you can see the how customer requests are distributed to relevant X tasks with the result of the stored in the S3 separately. So by this way, with following the monolithic architecture, it is basically reduced the infrastructure cost by over the 19%. It is really a huge number and it is increased the scaling capabilities and handling thousands of the streams with the capacity for more and also utilizing Amazon EC2 compute. Saving the plans further reduce the cost. If you scroll down and go to the result and take away, you can see that the infrastructure reduce cost by over the 19%. So that means the key decision from the team, Re-architecting from a distributed microservice approach to the monolithic architecture brought a significant improvements.
