chapters.text: In this section we are going to learn modular monolith architecture. Or we can say that modular architecture. And this is the bridge and the gateway between monolithic and microservice architectures. Let's start with what is modular monolith architecture? Modular monolith architecture is an approach where an application is developed as a single cohesive unit, but it is internally divided into well-defined, loosely coupled modules. This allows the application to maintain the simplicity of a monolithic while reaping some benefits of modularity, typically associated with the microservices. The modular monolith architecture breaks up the code into independent modules, and each module encapsulates their own features needed in your application, and each module only connect to other modules that specifically provide services that needs to it. So in module architecture, we still build and deploy a single application, which is same with a traditional monolithic architecture. But we build application with breaking up the code into independent modules for each of these features needed into your application. Modules are represent bounded context of our application domain, and in modules we group the features of the domain context. By this way, we reduce the dependencies of modules and we can develop or modify a module without affecting other modules. If you look at the key characteristics of the modular monolith architecture, we can say that the first key characteristic is modularity and bounded context. In modular monolith, the application is divided into several modules, each encapsulating a specific business domain. Modules communicate through well-defined interfaces, reducing dependencies and enhancing maintainability. The second characteristic is simplified deployment and operations. Unlike microservices, where each service may have its own deployment pipeline and environment, modular monoliths are deployed as a single unit. This reduces operational complexity and eliminates issues arising from the service interdependencies. The entire application is deployed as one unit, simplifying deployment and operations. Under another characteristic could be inter-module communication modules within a modular unit communicate via direct method calls or event mechanisms within the same application process. This eliminates the network latency associated with microservices and leading to better performance and simpler transaction management. And another key characteristic is unified data management. Although each module can operate independently, modular monoliths typically utilize a unified database architecture. This can be segmented by schemas or tables specific to each module, enabling ease of data management while maintaining the integrity and security of the data. In more advanced setups, separate databases may be used if the modules require complete isolation, but these configurations still maintain straightforward interfaces for inter-module interactions. Here you can find a preview of our Icap Modular Monolith application. Here you can see the modules are catalog, basket ordering and identity. In catalogue module we can manage product information including details, pricing and inventory. In basket microservices, we will handle shopping cart functionality including adding and updating items and removing items in the shopping cart. In the ordering module, we will manage the order processing workflow including the order creation flow and in identity module, we will provide user authentication and authorization services using the Keycloak. And if you look at the architecture overview, each module is developed as a separate class library project with its own data access, business logic and API layer, and the API project acts as an entrypoint, exposing endpoints that interact with the underlying modules and modules communicate with each other through well-defined interfaces and domain events. Or we can say public APIs or domain events. In this video, we are going to explore why modular monoliths are becoming increasingly popular and how they offer advantages over the microservices. And the first reason is industry evolution. As the industry continues to wrestle with the tradeoffs between monolithic and microservice architectures, a middle path has started to gain favor, which is the modular monolith architecture. This trend reflects a growing recognition of the need to balance the simplicity of monoliths with the flexibility of microservices. The modular monolith is not a new concept, but it is experiencing a resurgence as businesses seek efficient and scalable solutions without the complexities of microservices. Modular monoliths serve as a gateway between traditional monoliths and microservices. They combine the best aspects of both architectures, offering the balanced approach to modern application development. So if you summarize why modular Monoliths, we can say that modular monoliths attempt to harmonize the benefits of the monolithic architecture with the agility of the microservice architecture. And each module is designed around the business domain, akin to how the services are scoped in microservices. But unlike microservices, these modules are not separate services that communicate over a network but are part of the same application process. So now we can examine the advantages of the microservices. And the first advantage is simplified communication and network overhead. This architecture eliminates much of the complexity associated with the network communications in microservices, as all the components run within the same process. And the second advantages is unified data management and transactional integrity. Maintaining a unified database, or at least a coordinated database schema, simplifies the data management and ensure the transaction integrity. Also, it ensures data consistency without the need for distributed transaction management. And another advantage is effective scalability. While not as granular as in microservices, scalability can still be effectively managed through the modern hardware software load balancers by optimizing individual modules, and we can also perform the resource optimization in modular monoliths with allowing for targeted scaling of specific modules that require more resources. And another advantage we can say development simplicity. Running all components within the same process. Avoid the complexities of managing a distributed system such as service discovery, network latency, and inter service communications, and that simplifies the deployment and operations, reducing the risk of the failures and the need for extensive monitoring. In this video, we are going to learn monolithic first approaches from Martin Fowler and Sam Newman. First of all, let me start with Martin Fowler. Martin Fowler has article about monolithic first which written 2015. You can see the link on the slide and you can read by clicking this link. In this article, he said that almost all the successful microservices stories have started with the monolithic architecture that got too big and was broken up, and almost all the cases where the system that started and built as a microservice system from the scratch it is end up with serious troubles. So you shouldn't start any project with microservices, even if you are sure your application will be big enough to make it worthwhile. Microservices are useful architectures, but even their advocates say that using them incurs a significant complexity, which means they are only useful with more complex systems. The first reason for this is classic jargon. When you begin a new application, how sure are you that it will be useful to your users? The second issue is starting with microservices, is that they only work well if you come up with good, stable boundaries between the services, which is essentially the task of driving up the right setting to bounded context. So there are different ways to execute a monolithic first strategy. The logical way is to design a monolithic, carefully paying attention to modularity within the software, both at the API boundaries and how the data is stored. So these are the ideas of Martin Fowler about multi first approaches from his article. And if you continue the second slide you can see that there is also a second quotation from Sam Newman. Sam Newman has a book which is The Building microservices. Sam Newman in the book of the building microservices. He is agrees with the Martin Fowler approach, which is the recommend to beginning to development with the monolithic architecture, which should be the single deployable units. So he recommends to leveraging microservices only if you can become convinced of the benefits of the system, not start as a default for every project with microservices. And also, he said that a monolithic architecture is a choice and valid choice for most of the projects, and it is sensible default choice for architecture style as a Monte-Carlo architecture. In other words, looking for a reason to be convinced to use microservices rather than looking for a reason not to use them. In this video, we are going to learn what is starting point for microservices and gradually transition with incremental refactoring. We will explore how we can start with a modular monolith architecture and gradually transition to microservices. And the first point is modular monolith architecture. Modular monolith architecture provide a more evolutionary approach to software architecture, and organizations can start with a modular monolith approach, and as needs change, gradually spin off the certain modules as full fledged microservices, and this provides a greater flexibility. And this flexibility is a significant advantage for businesses navigating uncertain markets or those in the midst of the digital transformation. And also, we can apply incremental refactoring instead of a complete overhaul. Companies can incrementally refactor parts of the monolithic into microservices, reducing risk and disruption. And we will apply strategic migration by focusing on critical areas that benefits most from the microservices and ensuring efficient resource utilization. So now let's consider how we can gradually transition with incremental approach and incremental refactoring. The first thing we can do avoiding the Big Bang approach. Transitioning directly from a monolithic to microservices can be risky and complex. The Big Bang approach involves significant changes that can disrupt the entire system. For that reason, we can follow the incremental refactoring. Incremental refactoring allows you to gradually break down the monolithic into microservices, this approach reduces risk and allows for continuous delivery and ensures that the system remains stable throughout the transition. So the benefits of this approach is risk mitigation. Smaller, manageable change reduce the risk of introducing critical issues by focusing on one module or services at a time. And also it allows for ongoing improvements and adaptations based on the feedback and changing requirements. So now let's see the steps for incremental refactoring for an example of the Icap modular Mold application. So first of all, we will start with initial modular modular architecture and start with Icap module mold application that include modules like catalog, basket ordering, identity, and each module is part of a single deployment unit. After developing our application with following the modular mold approach, you can see the steps in here to transitioning incremental refactoring to microservices. And the first step is identify candidates. So we should begin by identifying modules or components within your monolith that can be isolated as microservices. So we should look for areas with clear boundaries and limited dependencies. And that means the second step is define clear boundaries for your modules or services. In this video we are going to learn vertical slice Architecture, vertical slice architecture introduced by Jaime Bogaerts. And it offers this architecture against two traditional layered onion clean architecture approaches. Vertical Slice Architecture aims to organize code around specific features or use cases, rather than the technical concerns. In a vertical slice architecture, a feature is implemented across all layers of the software, from the user interface to the database. This approach is often used in the development of complex, feature rich applications, particularly those that are delivered over the web. Vertical slice architecture is dividing an application into distinct features or functionalities, and each of them cuts through all of the layers of the application. This approach is in contrast to the traditional n-tier or layered architectures, where the application is divided horizontally. So that means, unlike the traditional n-tier or layered architectures where the application is divided horizontally. As you can see, the image, presentation, application and data layers, the vertical slice architectures divide an application vertically based on the features or functionalities. So let's see the characteristics of the vertical slice architecture. Of course, the first characteristic is. The application is divided into feature based slices. So the application is divided into slices based on the features or use cases. Each slice represents a complete vertical pathway through the application stack, including the user interface, business logic, and data access layers, and each slice is self-contained and independent. That means each vertical slice is intended to be self-contained and independent from other slices. This means that a single feature can be developed, tested, and deployed without impacting other parts of the application. There are reduced dependencies between different parts of the application. By focusing on the vertical slices, dependencies between different areas of the application are minimized. Each slice can be seen as a mini application with its own distinct functionality and dependencies. It promotes the use of the cross functional teams. Development teams working with the vertical slice architecture are often cross functional, containing all the skills necessary to develop a feature from start to finish, which promotes a more holistic view of the development, and the architecture supports scalability and maintainability. This architecture allows for better scalability and maintainability. As the application grows, new features can be added as a knife slices without significantly impacting the existing code, and also it allows for improved testing and deployment process. Vertical slices can be individually tested and deployed, which simplifies the testing process, and it can lead to more reliable and faster deployments. Vertical slice is an internal application architecture pattern that every microservices can follow this architecture in their internal code. Organizations In the microservices, every microservices handle a specific piece of the functionality and communicate with other services through well-defined interfaces. So let's see advantages of the vertical slice architecture. Vertical slice architecture offers several benefits. It allows for focused development where the teams can concentrate on one feature at a time. It simplifies refactoring and upgrades, since changes in one slice usually don't affect others. It aligns well with the agile and DevOps practices, supporting incremental development and continuous delivery. However, there are also challenges with using the vertical slice. You might encounter duplication of code across slices, particularly for common functionalities. There is a learning curve involved, especially for teams opposed to the traditional architectures. The design of each slice requires careful consideration in order to ensure independence and maintainability. So vertical slice architecture is a powerful approach for developing complex applications where modularity and rapid development are crucial. So we will also use vertical slice architecture into our catalog and basket microservices. In this video we are going to learn clean architecture. Clean architecture introduced by Robert C Martin, also known as uncle Bob. It is a software architecture design that aims to separate concerns and create systems that are independent of the frameworks, UI and databases. This approach leads to systems that are more maintainable, flexible and adaptable to changes. So let's check the key principles of the clean architecture. The first key principle is independence of frameworks. The system is not tightly coupled with a specific framework, which makes it adaptable to changes in frameworks and tools. And the second principle is testable. The business rules can be tested without the UI. Database, web server or any external element. And another key principle is UI agnostic UI independence. The UI can change easily without changing the rest of the system. The architecture can be unplugged from the user interface. And another key principle is database agnostic, which means database independence. The business rules are not bound to the database. The architecture is decoupled from the underlying data store. And another key principle is external system agnostic, external agency independence. Business rules do not know anything about the outside of the world. The business rules of the architecture are isolated and know nothing about the outside of the world. And now let's check the clean architecture layers. The first layer is entities layer. Or we can say domain layer. Domain layer represents the business objects of the application. It is the core of the system and includes enterprise wide logic and business rules. It contains enterprise wide business rules and entities encapsulate the most general and high level rules. As an example, we can give ordering microservices. Entities might be ordered and order item, and the second layer is use case layer, which we can say application layer. It contains application specific business rules, and it encapsulates and implements all of the use cases of the system. As an example of the application layer use cases, we can say that create order, cancel order and update order status use case for ordering microservices. And another layer is interface adapter layers which is the infrastructure layer. It basically converts data from the format most convenient for the use cases and entities to the format most convenient for external systems. As an example, we can give mapping data from database models to the domain entities. And the last layer we can say that frameworks and drivers. That means infrastructure external concerns the outermost layer consisting of the frameworks and tools such as the database, the web framework and so on. So generally includes the UI, database, external interfaces and so on. As an example, we can say that Rest controllers and database repositories. So as a conclusion, we can say that clean architecture in microservices lead to system that are more modular, adaptable, testable and maintainable. We say modular because changes in one module or service don't heavily impact others, and we say adaptable because it is easier to update or replace technologies and databases. And we say testable because core logic can be tested in isolation. And lastly, we say maintainable because clear separation of concerns makes the system easier to understand and maintain. In this video we are going to compare vertical slice architecture versus clean architecture. Vertical slice architecture emphasizes organizing software development around the features, cutting through all layers in a vertical manner. Each feature or slice is developed and deployed independently. On the other hand, clean architecture focuses on separation of concerns and dependency rules. It organizes the code into layers with the most stable and business agnostic layers at the center of the entities, and use cases and external concerns like UI and data access layers on the outer layers with vertical slice architecture. Development teams concentrate on delivering complete features, each potentially touching all layers of the stack. It is closely aligned with the agile methodologies where delivering working, testable features rapidly is a key. But the clean architecture advocates for a more structured approach, ensuring that business logic is decoupled from the external concerns. This approach is beneficial for long term maintainability and scalability. Vertical slice architecture shines in scenarios where the rapid development and delivery of the independent features are critical. It is well suited for agile teams working on complex applications, with the numerous features that may change or evolve independently. Clean architecture is ideal for large scaled applications, where long term maintenance and scalability and the ability to adapt to changing business requirements or technologies are paramount. It ensures that core business logic remains unaffected by changes in the external layers. So when to choose vertical slice over the clean architecture. The first reason is rapid development and deployment. When the priority is to develop and deploy features rapidly and independently, vertical slice is more appropriate. The second reason is agile and scrum teams. For teams practicing the agile or scrum that need to deliver complete features in short cycles, vertical Slice aligns well with these methodologies. So after creating all these modules, we will continue with the shared folders. In this video we are going to learn shared kernel pattern in domain driven design. What is the shared kernel pattern? The shared kernel pattern is a concept in domain driven design, where a small subset of the domain model is shared between multiple bounded contexts or modules. This shared subset typically includes common data structures, utility classes, or services that multiple parts of the system need to use, and the shared kernel ensures consistency and reduce duplication while maintaining the clear boundaries between different modules. So why we use the shared kernel pattern into our module module architecture? The first reason is consistency. By sharing a common subset of the domain model, we ensure consistency across different modules. This is particularly important for entities or value objects that are used in multiple modules. And the second reason is reduce duplication. Instead of duplicating common code in multiple modules, the shared kernel allows us to centralize this code in one place and reducing redundancy and the risk of the inconsistencies. And another reason is decoupling. While the sharing kernel promotes sharing, it also enforces clear boundaries between modules. Only the minimal necessary part of the domain module are shared and keeping the rest of the module implementation decoupled. So with applying these shared kernel pattern, we ensure consistency, reduce duplication, and maintain the clear boundaries between our modules while allowing them to remain decoupled and maintainable in our modular monolith architecture. chapters.text: In this section, we will begin our journey with the first module in our modular monolith architecture, which is the catalog module. We will explore how to develop the catalog module using the vertical slice architecture and the sequence design pattern. Here you can see the our big picture of the project. We are at this point and we will develop catalog module. We will start by domain analysis of the catalog module, understanding the modules, use case Rest APIs and database. And after that we will do technical analysis of the catalog module to understanding the architecture pattern libraries and so on. So here you can see the internal architecture of the catalog module that we are going to follow. Vertical slice architecture domain driven design and secure pattern. Also we will use these NuGet packages when implementing internal catalog module architecture with connecting to each DB as opposed to SQL database. So we can say that we will learn vertical slice architecture implementation with the feature folders and we will learn domain driven design, secure design pattern, logical and physical implementation of the security pattern. Security design pattern with using the mediator library, Entity framework, core connection with the PostgreSQL database, and minimal APIs for building the first HTTP APIs that we are going to cover in this section. In this video we are going to do domain analysis of catalog module. We will explore these domain analysis in four key areas. These are the domain modules, application use cases, Rest API endpoints and underlying data structures. So now let's start with the first key area which is the domain modules and entities. Our primary domain module is product and associated with the category. A product has attributes like ID, name, category, description, image, file and price. And here you can see the image on the slide, the product model, entity and relation with the category. And we will also consider the domain events for the product created and product price change event and leading to integration events. So the next step is we will analyze application use cases or main use cases are Crud operations including the listing products and categories. Get product with product ID. Get products with category. Create, update and delete products and also when updating a product. If the price has changed, it will raise a product price change domain event that will lead to integration event. And along with these use cases, we will design our APIs according to this perspective. So in the next step we will rest API endpoint analysis for catalog module. And here you can see the Rest APIs for the catalog module. According to these Rest API we will expose HTTP get method for the slash products endpoint. This will be covered or list all product use cases and we will also expose HTTP get method for slash products, slash ID for fetching a specific product. And also we will expose get method for products slash category in order to get products by category use case. And we have also http post method to expose slash products endpoint in order to create any product and we will expose HTTP put method for slash products slash ID in order to update a specific product. And lastly, we will expose HTTP delete for slash products slash ID in order to remove a product. So these endpoints will be implemented using ASP.Net core API project, where each action corresponds to a specific HTTP method and URI. So the next step we will analyze underlying data structures of catalog module. The underlying data structure for the catalog module will be stored in a relational database. And we will use PostgreSQL or database. That means we will use a single ish of relational database, specifically PostgreSQL, and we will create a schema specifically, for example, catalog schema within the PostgreSQL database for data isolation of the catalog module. And we will use Entity Framework core code first approach for database migrations and entity configurations. And we will configure our entities and their relationships using the Entity Framework core, ensuring that our database structure aligned with our domain models. In this video we are going to do technical analysis of catalog module. These include application architecture, style pattern and principles libraries, NuGet packages and project folder structure. So let's start with the application architecture style. As an application architecture style we will use vertical slice architecture and this approach. Organize our code into feature folders, with each feature folder encapsulated in a single C-sharp file containing all necessary classes. And if you look at the pattern and principles, we can say that we will implement secure design pattern queries, which means command query responsibility segregation divides operations into commands and queries. Or we can say that write and read that, promoting the flexibility and scalability. And another pattern is mediator pattern that facilitate object interactions through a mediator reducing direct dependencies and simplifying the communications, especially in complex workflows. And also we will use dependency injection in ASP.Net core. Dependency injection is a core feature allowing us to inject dependencies rather than hardcoding them and enhancing the maintainability and testability, and we will use minimal APIs and routing in ASP.Net. ASP.Net eight minimal APIs, simplifying the endpoint definitions and providing lightweight syntax for routing and handling HTTP requests. And we will also use Entity Framework Core or Run Tool pattern in this pattern. Object relational mapping abstracts the database interactions, enabling us to work with the database objects using the high level programming constructs, simplifying the data manipulation. And after that we will continue with the libraries and NuGet packages that we are going to use in catalog module. We will use mediator for securities. Mediator library simplifies the implementation of the securities design pattern by acting as an in-process messaging mechanism, helping to send commands and queries to their respective handlers. And also we will use Carter for API endpoints. Carter offers a lightweight library for routing and handling HTTP requests, making it easier to define API endpoints with clean and concise code. And also we will use Entity Framework core PostgreSQL interaction. Entity Framework Core provide a robust ORM for interacting with PostgreSQL, enabling us to manage database operations with ease, and also we will use map setter for object mapping maps. There is a fast, configurable object mapper that simplifies the task of mapping objects from one type to another, streamlining the data transformation process. And also we will use fluent validation for input validation. Fluent Validation Library is a popular library for building strongly typed validation rules, ensuring our inputs are correct before they are processed by the application. And next we will examine the project folder structure. You can see project folder structure for catalog module on the slide. The project is organized into data feature folders and modules feature folders stored under the products features like Create Product and Get Product have dedicated handlers and endpoint definitions. Feature folder will be products for the catalog module data folder and context objects managed database interactions. So you can see the image on the slide. This is the catalog module folder structure that we are going to develop one by one data folder consisting for the data operations, configurations, migrations and see the operations using the Entity Framework core. And the product is a feature folder. Under this feature folder we have a features and under features we will implement application use cases including the create product, Delete product, Get Product by category, Get Product by ID, Get Products and Update Product and each feature folder including endpoint definition and the handler class. And these will be following the vertical slice architecture. And we will also have modules folder in order to represent our domain model. And these will be representing our catalog folder structure that we will develop during the course. In this video we are going to learn way of development. We will outline the development path for our Icap Modular Monolith project. Our project is organized into three main folders. These are the shared modules and API folders. And here you can see the way of development path. Overview of our development path is structured to ensure we build a robust and scalable application. And the path will be start with the shared and continue with the modules. And lastly we will develop API folders. So that means we will start by developing shared common classes, abstractions and utilities that we will use the across multiple modules. And after develop these common operations in the shared module we will continue to develop modules folder using the shared components. We will develop each module one by one, ensuring each module is self-contained and adheres to all architectural principles. And after develop the all modules, we will contain the API folder. Finally, we will integrate all modules into the API project, exposing the Rest APIs to interact with the system. Here you can see the detailed development path that we are going to follow when developing our application. We will begin by creating our shared components. This will include common classes. utilities, abstractions, cross-cutting concerns. Here you can see the class library project. We will start by developing shared class library. These components provide the foundational building blocks for our modules to ensure consistency and reducing the duplication. And the second step we will develop modules. Each module will be developed using the following path. So underlying the modules we will follow the sub path which is the domain infrastructure application and presentation. So this is the sub steps for each module that we are going to follow when developing our modules. So in the domain operation we will start with defining domain entities, domain events and domain rules. And these layers represent the core business logic and the rules for specific module. And after that we will continue with the infrastructure operations. We will implement the infrastructure task which include database interactions using the Entity Framework, core migrations and entity configurations. And after that we will continue to develop application use cases. We will then develop the application use cases, which involves the business logic using the patterns like SQS and mediator. These application use case orchestrate the business rules and data access. And finally, we will develop and expose the modules functionality through the Rest APIs that enabling external systems to interact with our application. So these are the sub steps for each module under the modules folder. After developing the all modules, we will contain the API project that we are going to expose RESTful APIs using the ASP.Net controllers. So these are the way of development of our course. During the course we will follow these way of development and complete all these class libraries and API project for implementing the modular monolith architecture. So now let's start our development journey with the shared folders. This is the foundational elements that other modules will depend on, and we will create shared class library and abstractions that we will use from the modules. And we will implement cross-cutting concerns.

chapters.text: In this section, we are going to develop the shared domain layer for tactical domain driven design abstractions. So we will cover the foundational concepts and practical steps to implement the key domain driven design patterns, such as the entities, value objects, aggregates and the domain events. So as you can see that we are at this point and we are going to develop shared class library with using the tactical domain driven design pattern as an abstraction. So that means in this section we will cover the domain driven design. Tactical domain driven design entities, value objects, aggregates and aggregate routes. And we will develop domain driven design abstractions with developing entity aggregate and domain events. And we will develop high domain event interface in tactical domain driven design abstraction using the mediator identification. And we will develop aggregate classes that includes domain events using the mediator I notification interface. In this video we are going to learn Domain-driven design. So what is domain driven design? Domain driven design has been around since Eric Evans published his book about the subject in 2003. Domain driven design is not an improved technology or specific method. Domain driven design is. Solving a complex problem is to break the problem into smaller parts and focus on the smaller problems that are relatively easy. A complex domain may contain subdomains, and some of the subdomains can combine and grouping with each other for common rules and responsibilities. So domain driven design has two types. These are the strategic and tactical domain driven design. Strategic domain driven design is about understanding and modeling the business domain. It involves identifying the different domains, their subdomains, and how they interact with each other. In tactical domain driven design is more about the implementation details and provide design patterns including the patterns like entities, value objects, aggregates, and so on. For this video, we will focus on the strategic domain driven design and it is key concepts in order to understand domain driven design, some basic concepts need to be mastered and these are the domain subdomain, ubiquitous language, bounded context and context mapping. So let me start with the domain. The domain is the spur of the knowledge and activity around which the application logic revolves. These are the entities, value objects, aggregates, and aggregate rules. An entity is an object that identified by its identity rather than its attributes. The identity makes each entity unique even if other attributes are the same. Entities are used to represent objects in the system that have a distinct identity and life cycle. They often correspond to real world objects that need to be tracked or managed. As an example, we can give ordering microservices. An order can be an entity. It is unique identified by an order ID, even if the other attributes are identical to another order. A value object is an object that describes some characteristics or attributes, but carries no concepts of the identity. Value objects are used to describe aspects of the domain with no conceptual identity. They are immutable and are often used to encapsulate complex attributes. An anemic domain model is characterized by entities that have little or no business logic.
